California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
R. Clifford Lober 
County Counsel 
Napa County 
1195 Third st., Room 301 
Napa, CA 
94559-3001 
Dear Mr. Lober: 
January 6, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal 
Assistance; Our File 
No. 1-87-267 
You have requested advice concerning the responsibilities 
of Napa County Planning Commission Members will Nord, Guy Kay 
and Linda McGrew under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act.1/ 
Because your request is more of a 
general inquiry than a request for advice as to a specific 
action pending before the planning commission, we treat your 
request as one for informal assistance.~/ 
QUESTIONS 
Do the economic interests of Planning Commission Members 
Nord, Kay and McGrew in the wine industry require their 
disqualification from participation in the following planning 
commission decisions: 
(1) 
creating a legal definition of the term "winery"; and 
(2) 
Requiring use permits, environmental impact reports, 
or other regulation of operations or improvements of wineries? 
1/ Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California 
Administrative Code section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California 
Administrative Code. 
~/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with 
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. 
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.) 
428 J Street, Suite 800 .. P.O. Box 807 ~ Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916) 322~5660 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 2 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. 
All three planning commissioners must disqualify 
themselves from decisions regarding the definition of 
"winery." 
Should definitional issues become more narrowly 
focused, allowing for a more individual analysis of the 
foreseeable effects on the planning commissioners' economic 
interests, it is possible that some participation may be 
allowed in the future. 
2. 
All three planning commissioners must disqualify 
themselves from any decisions regarding use permits, 
environmental impact reports, or regulation of operation or 
improvements for their employers, sources of income or business 
interests. 
Additionally, they must disqualify themselves from 
decisions involving a competing winery or vintner when the 
competitor is the subject of a decision which could foreseeably 
have a material financial effect on the individual 
commissioner's economic interests. 
FACTS 
Napa County has a population of approximately 102,000 
people, and a total land area of 513,000 acres of land, most of 
which is mountainous. 
Napa's wine industry is world renowned 
and is situated in a narrow corridor encompassing 11% of the 
county's land. 
There are 154 operating wineries in the 
county. 
The wine industry, along with the one-mil 1 ion-plus 
tourists that it brings to the area, constitutes a major 
economic resource for the county. 
Three of the five planning commission members have ties to 
the wine industry: 
will Nord -- Vice-president in charge of viticultural 
operations at Domaine Chandon, a major facility with a 
restaurant, gift shop and musical entertainment located within 
the unincorporated area of the county. 
Mr. Nord is also a 33% 
owner of a winery and vineyard located in the county. 
Guy Kay -- Vice-president in charge of production 
operations of Beringer Winery. 
Beringer owns vineyards within 
the unincorporated area of the county. 
Beringer produces its 
wines at its winery in the City of st. Helena, which is outside 
the jurisdiction of the county. 
Mr. Kay receives a salary and 
participates in a plan for extra compensation to executives 
which is not conditioned upon any particular level of profits. 
Linda McGrew -- Her husband is an independent contractor 
who is under contract to publish the Domaine Chandon Quarterly 
for the Domaine Chandon winery. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 3 
The matters concerning wineries that come before the 
planning commission include: 
1. Use permits for the construction or expansion of 
wineries. 
The planning commission is the final authority, 
absent an appeal to the board of supervisors. 
2. Determinations regarding whether to require 
environmental impact reports. 
3. possible imposition of conditions for operation of and 
improvements to wineries. 
4. Establishing the legal definition of 
"winery." 
Historically, the Napa County zoning code has permitted the 
operation of a winery in either the agriculture-watershed (AW) 
zone or in the agricultural preserve (AP) zone. 
The current 
zoning ordinance has, for years, defined a winery as a facility 
for the processing and storage of grapes and appurtenant 
facilities and uses. 
Under this rather broad definition, wineries have 
flourished in the Napa Valley and have engaged in numerous 
"appurtenant uses," including conducting tours, operating 
restaurants and delicatessens, providing picnic supplies and 
facilities, and selling souvenirs and memorabilia, not to 
mention the sale of wines. 
Currently pending before the planning commission is a 
proposal for a revised definition of permitted winery uses 
within the AW and AP zones. 
This proposal includes a 
requirement that some or all of the winery-related uses 
described above would be permitted only upon obtaining a 
conditional use permit. 
Among the various possible versions of the use permit 
requirement are proposals to "grandfather" in those uses which 
are currently in effect at the various wineries. 
Thus, those 
uses would be permitted to continue without the necessity of 
applying for a use permit. 
However, any future new uses of 
this type would be subject to the use permit requirement. 
Another version would require wineries with all such uses, 
whether ongoing or not, to apply for a use permit. 
If denied, 
the current use would be required to stop. 
It is also possible 
that the current use would be permitted, but would be 
conditioned on other requirements such as additional parking. 
A third version would "grandfather in" the basic winery 
operations, but require use permits for any of the 
"appurtenant" uses. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 4 
Lastly, wine grape growers have proposed that the 
definition for a winery operating in these two zones include a 
requirement that at least 75% of all grapes utilized at the 
winery be grown in Napa County. 
In addition, under this 
proposed definition, no bottled or bulk wines could be brought 
into the county for use by Napa County wineries. 
The growers 
learned of a similar provision in the Santa Barbara County 
zoning ordinance and seek its inclusion in the revised Napa 
County ordinance. 
Vintners, on the other hand, have expressed opposition to 
the inclusion of these restrictions on their operations. 
They 
often use out-of-county grapes or bulk wines in the production 
of their blended or non-premium wines. 
Because these wines 
constitute a major portion of the wines made by Napa County 
wineries, the total percentage of out-of-county grapes may 
easily exceed 25%. 
In addition, several of the wineries store 
the wines which they have produced in Napa County in storage 
facilities outside the county. 
This is done for economic 
reasons. 
The vintners oppose a restriction on bringing this 
wine back into the county at a later date. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, 
participating in, or using their official positions to 
influence a governmental decision in which they know or have 
reason to know they have a financial interest. 
An official has 
a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on the official or a member of the official's 
immediate family, or on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than gifts and 
other than loans by a commercial lending institution 
in the regular course of business on terms available 
to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more 
in value provided to, received by or promised to the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 5 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
section 87103(a)-(d). 
All the planning commissioners are public officials, as 
defined in section 82048. 
Each of the three in question has 
one or more economic interests which could be materially 
affected by a decision of the planning commission. 
Mr. Nord is a vice-president with Domaine Chandon; thus 
Domaine Chandon is a source of income of $250 or more and a 
business entity in which he holds a position of management. 
(Sections 82005 and 82030.) 
Additionally, his 33-percent 
interest in a winery and vineyard in the county are both an 
investment interest in a business entity and an interest in 
real property which we assume are worth at least $1,000 each. 
(Sections 82033 and 82034.) 
Mr. Kay is vice-president in charge of production 
operations for Beringer Winery; thus the winery is a source of 
income of $250 or more and a business entity in which he holds 
a position of management. 
(Sections 82005 and 82030.) 
Ms. McGrew's husband's contractual relationship with 
Domaine Chandon makes Domaine Chandon a source of income to 
her, based on her community property share of the gross income 
received or promised. 
(Section 82030(a).) 
If her husband's 
gross income from Domaine Chandon was $500 or more in the past 
year, Domaine Chandon is a source of income of $250 or more to 
Ms. McGrew. 
In light of these various financial relationships, each of 
these public officials would be required to disqualify himself 
or herself if a decision of the planning commission would have 
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his or 
her economic interests, and the effect is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. 
Question #1. 
Foreseeability 
We will examine first whether the planning commissioners 
may vote on the definition of "winery." 
As was noted 
previously, public officials may not participate in 
governmental decisions which could foreseeably have a material 
financial effect on their economic interests in a manner 
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
The 
effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 6 
sUbstantial likelihood that it will occur. 
Certainty is not 
required; however, if the effect is a mere possibility, it is 
not reasonably foreseeable. 
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
198, copy enclosed.) 
Deciding on the legal definition of "winery", and including 
therein any necessary language regarding the issuance of use 
permits, is an issue that will have a direct impact on the 
financial future of the growers and wineries in the county. 
Each of the proposals submitted for consideration to the 
planning commission would have a foreseeable effect on the 
economic interests of the three commissioners. 
Material Financial Effect 
It is necessary next to determine if the decisions 
regarding the definition of winery will materially affect the 
planning commissioners' economic interests. 
The effect of a 
decision is material if it is "significant." 
(Regulation 
18702(a), copy enclosed.) 
Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) 
contains monetary guidelines for determining whether the effect 
of a decision will be material. 
The test of materiality varies 
with the financial size of the business entity in which the 
public official has an economic interest. 
For example, an 
effect of only $10,000 on the gross revenues or assets of a 
small business is material, while a $1 million effect on the 
gross revenues or assets of a Fortune 500 company is material. 
The businesses in which the planning commissioners have 
economic interests range from Mr. Nord's local winery and 
vineyard, to Domaine Chandon which is a multi-million dollar 
international corporation. 
While we are not in the position to determine the precise 
magnitude of the effect of these decisions, a significant 
effect is likely because the ramifications of the various 
options are so substantial. 
For example, the proposal that the 
definition for winery include a requirement that at least 75% 
of all grapes utilized by wineries located in the county be 
grown in Napa County would greatly benefit Beringer, whose 
grapes, grown in the county, would be more in demand. 
The 
value of Beringer's assets would increase under such a 
definition. 
At the same time, this recommended definition 
would have a negative impact on Domaine Chandon because the 
winery's current practice of utilizing grapes from other 
regions for wine production would be severely limited. 
However, how much this would specifically affect Domaine 
Chandon's assets or increase its expenses is a determination 
you will have to make. 
On the other hand, the proposed "grandfathering" provision 
for wineries with "appurtenant uses" would greatly benefit 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 7 
Domaine Chandon because of the many attractions currently in 
operation at the winery. 
Those wineries with little or no 
extraneous activity would be at a distinct disadvantage due to 
the proposed use permit requirement, while Domaine Chandon's. 
assets would increase in value. 
In light of the significant effect of a new definition of 
winery, we must conclude that the decision regarding the 
definition of winery will have a material financial effect on 
the economic interests of the planning commissioners. 
However, as the discussion regarding the definition of 
winery becomes more focused, the effects of the provisions 
under consideration will be more easily identified. 
At that 
time, it would be advisable to re-evaluate the specific effect 
of the proposed legislation on the economic interests of the 
individual planning commissioners, and apply the appropriate 
guidelines of Regulation 18702.2. 
It is possible that the 
language in the definition under discussion at that time would 
not have a material effect on each of the economic interests of 
the planning commissioners. 
Public Generally Exception 
The final question to be answered in determining whether 
the planning commissioners face a conflict-of-interest 
situation is whether the decisions regarding the definition of 
winery will affect their financial interests in a manner that 
is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Regulation 18703 (copy enclosed) provides that a material 
financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's 
interests is distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally unless the decision will affect the official's 
economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will 
affect all members of the public or a significant segment of 
the public. 
The "public," for purposes of this exception, 
includes the residents and businesses located within the 
jurisdiction of the planning commission. 
(See In re Owen 
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copy enclosed.) 
Thus, for purposes of 
the planning commission, the public includes all residents and 
businesses in the county. 
The decisions regarding the definition of "winery" clearly 
do not have the same or similar effect on the wine industry as 
they do on the general public. 
However, because the wine 
industry is a major industry in Napa County, one might assume 
that it constitutes a "significant segment of the public". 
Regulation 18703 specifically defines when an industry is a 
significant segment of the public: 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 8 
... Except as provided herein, an industry, trade or 
profession does not constitute a significant segment 
of the general public. 
(a) 
In the case of an elected state officer, an 
industry, trade or profession constitutes a 
significant segment of the public generally. 
(b) 
In the case of any other elected official, 
an industry, trade or profession of which that 
official is a member may constitute a significant 
segment of the public generally if that industry, 
trade or profession is a predominant industry, trade 
or profession in the official's jurisdiction or in the 
district represented by the official. 
(c) 
An industry, trade or profession constitutes 
a significant segment of the public if the statute, 
ordinance or other provision of law which creates or 
authorizes the creation of the official's agency or 
office contains a finding and declaration, including 
an express reference to section 87103 of the 
Government Code, to the following effect: 
The Legislature [or other authority] declares 
that the individual[s] appointed to the office 
of 
is [are] intended to represent 
and further the interest of the [specified 
industry, trade or profession], and that such 
representation and furtherance will ultimately 
serve the public interest. 
ACcordingly, the 
Legislature [or other authority] finds that for 
purposes of persons who hold such office the 
[specified industry, trade or profession] is 
tantamount to and constitutes the public 
generally within the meaning of Section 87103 of 
the Government Code. 
(d) 
In the absence of an express finding and 
declaration of the type described in sUbsection (c) of 
this section, such an industry, trade or profession 
constitutes a significant segment of the public 
generally only if such a finding and declaration is 
implicit, taking into account the language of the 
statute, ordinance or other provision of law creating 
or authorizing the creation of the agency, the nature 
and purposes of the program, any applicable 
legislative history, and any other relevant 
circumstance. 
As you can see, Regulation 18703(a) applies to only state 
elected officials, and sUbsection (b) applies only to other 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 9 
elected officials. 
Since the planning commissioners serve in 
appointive positions, these provisions do not apply to them. 
Moreover, the "predominant industry" language in Regulation 
18703(b) is generally reserved for the "company town" 
situation. (See, Holmer Advice Letter, No. A-86-051, copy 
enclosed. ) 
Finally, the ordinance establishing the planning commission 
does not include specific language or implicit findings that it 
is in the public interest to appoint planning commissioners 
with expertise in wine-related fields. 
Planning commissions 
are not typically the kind of agency envisioned for the 
exceptions allowed in Regulations 18704(c) and (d). 
These 
provisions are intended to apply to boards and commissions 
created for the purpose of furthering the interests of a 
particular industry, trade or profession. 
(See Consumers Union 
of united States v. California Milk producers-Advisory Board 
(1978) 82 Cal. App. 3d 433.) 
Therefore, the "public 
generally" exception does not apply, and disqualification is 
required. 
Question #2. 
The second question involves the planning commissioners' 
participation in decisions regarding environmental impact 
reports, use permits or other issues specific to individual 
wineries, vineyards or businesses within the jurisdiction of 
the planning commission. 
Where the decision in question 
involves a business in which the public official has an 
economic interest, the public official's participation is 
addressed in Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed), which states 
in pertinent part: 
... a public official shall not make, participate in 
making, or use his or her official position to 
influence a government decision if: 
(1) 
Any person (including a business 
entity) which has been a source of income 
(including gifts) to the official of $250 or more 
in the preceding 12 months appears before the 
official in connection with the decision; 
(2) 
Any business entity in which the 
official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$1,000 or more, or in which the official is an 
officer, director, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management, appears before 
the official in connection with the decision; 
**** 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 10 
(b) 
A person or business entity appears before 
an official in connection with a decision when that 
person or entity either personally or by an agent: 
(1) 
Initiates the proceeding in which the 
decision will be made by filing an application, 
claim, appeal or similar request; 
(2) 
Is a named party in the proceeding 
concerning the decision before the official or 
the body on which the official serves. 
Regulation 18702.1. 
Thus, as to the type of decisions by the planning 
commission which involves an action related to a specific 
winery, such as the granting or denying of a use permit or 
imposing operating conditions, all three of the planning 
commissioners would have to disqualify themselves where their 
employer, source of income or investment interest appears 
before them. 
Another potential for disqualification arises where a 
competing winery or vintner appears before the planning 
commission seeking a use permit or other specific action. 
Because the entities seeking action or named in a proceeding 
are not economically tied to the public official, the 
provisions of Regulation 18702.1 do not apply to this type of 
situation. 
In such circumstances, the planning commissioners must 
determine whether the decision regarding the competitor will 
have a foreseeable material financial effect on their 
individual economic interests. 
The guidelines provided in 
Regulation 18702.2 and the analysis discussed under Question 
#1, above, would have to be used to determine whether there is 
a conflict-of-interest situation. 
(See also, Kay Advice 
Letter, No. A-83-157, copy enclosed.) 
Legally Required Participation 
The planning commission is made up of five members. 
Because three of the five planning commissioners have 
disqualifying financial interests regarding the decision on 
defining winery, their disqualification from participation 
leaves you without a quorum. 
section 87101 and Regulation 
18701 (copy enclosed) permit a public official to participate 
in a decision although he or she has a financial interest, but 
only when the official's participation is legally required and 
there exists no alternative source for the decision. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988 
Page 11 
In order to take action on the winery definition issue, 
three of the five planning commissioners must participate. 
Accordingly, participation by one of the three disqualified 
planning commissioners is legally required. 
(See In re Huds.on 
(1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13. 
See also Advice Letters to Hopkins 
A-82-088, Rainville A-85-024, and Levinger A-87-061, copies 
enclosed. ) 
It is important to emphasize that the rule of "legally 
required participation" under the Act is more strict than the 
common law "rule of necessity". 
As was noted in In re Hudson, 
supra: 
While the common law rule of necessity is analogous to 
the concept of "legally required participation" 
contained in the Political Reform Act, we believe that 
the purposes of the Act are best served by a rule 
which minimizes participation in government decisions 
by officials with a conflict of interest. 
In re Hudson, supra at p 17. 
In accordance with the requirements of the Act, you may 
select only the number of disqualified public officials legally 
necessary to take action. 
In this case, three of the five 
planning commissioners constitute a quorum, which is the legal 
requirement for decision-making by the planning commission. 
A 
method of random selection, such as drawing lots, is the 
preferred method for deciding which one of the three 
disqualified councilmembers may participate in these decisions. 
I trust this response provides sufficient guidance 
regarding the responsibilities of the planning commissioners 
under the Act. 
If, in the future, you have questions 
concerning the specific components of these important issues 
facing the planning commission, please feel free to contact me 
at (916) 322-5901. 
DMG:LS:plh 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
al 
Division 


NAPA COUNTY 
R. CLIFFORD lOBER County Counsel 
ROBERT WESTMEYER, Chief Deputy

DAVID l. ZALTSMAN. Deputy 
NANCY STUART GRISHAM. Deputy 
LAURA STIIT SHEEDY. Deputy 
j 
OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL 
1195 THIRD STREET, ROOM 301 • NAPA. CALIFORNIA 94559-3001 
AREA CODE 7071253-4521 
October 9 t 
1987 
OUR FILE NO.. 22 28 . 22 1 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Re: 
Request for an Opinion 
Gentlemen: 
This office requests an opinion pursuant to Government Code 
Section 83114(b) as to the duty of certain Napa County Planning 
Commissioners to refrain from voting under the following 
conditions. 
At a regular meeting on October 7, 1987, the subject commissioners 
consented to this office requesting this opinion. 
Napa County is famous throughout the world for 
i~s wine industry. 
The total population of Napa County is approximately 102,000. 
Napa County is small, with a total of 513,000 acres of land, most 
of which is mountainous. 
Most of the wine industry is found in a 
narrow corridor along Hwy. 29 and the Los Carneros area in the 
southwestern part of the County. 
This area encompasses only 11% 
of the County. 
Within this smaller area, there are now 154 
operating wineries of all sizes with several more either under 
construction or requesting approval. 
The wine industry, along 
with the one million plus tourists that it brings to this area, 
constitutes a major economic resource for the County. 
Many 
wineries own their own vineyards in the valley, others buy grapes 
from local vineyards. 
Three of the five commissioners have ties to the wine industry. 
1. 
Will Nord -- Vice-President in charge of Viticultural 
Operations at Domaine Chandon, a major sparkling wine 
facility, with a restaurant, gift shop and musical 
entertainment located within the unincorporated area of 
the County of Napa. 
Mr. Nord receives a salary from 
Domaine Chandon. 


Fair Practices 
ommission 
October 9, 198 
Page 2 
Mr. Nord is also a 33% interest owner of a winery and 
vineyard located within the unincorporated area of the 
County, but does not participate in management. 
2. 
Guy Kay -- Vice-President in charge of Production 
Operations of Berringer Winery. 
Berringer's corporate 
business name is Wine World, Incorporated. They produce 
various wines at their winery within the city limits of 
the City of St. Helena, thus not subject to County 
regulations, but they own vineyards within the 
unincorporated area of the County. 
Mr. Kay receives a 
salary and participates in a plan for extra 
compensation to executives. 
This extra compensation is 
not made conditioned upon any particular level of 
profits. 
3. 
Linda McGrew is not employed by a winery, but her 
husband, as an independent contractor, publishes the 
Domaine Chandon Quarterly for the Domaine Chandon 
Winery. 
The Quarterly only applies to Domaine Chandon. 
The matters concerning wineries that come before the commissioners 
are as follows: 
1. 
Use permits for the construction or expansion of 
wineries. 
The Planning Commission is the final 
authority in this matter absent an appeal to the Board 
of Supervisors. 
In very few cases are there appeals 
from winery approvals. 
2. 
The commissioners make the determination regarding the 
requirement of an Environmental Impact Report and 
impose operating conditions and regulate improvements 
on wineries. 
3. 
The definition of a winery is a specific matter soon to 
come before the Commission. 
The definition will 
address to what extent other activities such as sales 
of wine-related equipment, gifts, and souvenirs would 
be permitted, and whether entertainment and restaurants 
or other food sales will be permitted as a part of a 
winery. These ancillary activities are a source of 
substantial profits. 
The Board of Supervisors must 
approve any definition. 
While it is the position of the County that the particular 
expertise of these commissioners constitutes an asset to the 
decision-making process, it raises the issue that the very small 
geographical area, the small population of Napa County, and the 
heavy concentration of wineries and winery-related activities 


Fair Practices Commission 
October 9 I 
1987 
Page 3 
makes it probable that the financial interests of corporations of 
which the commissioners are officers could be affected by their 
votes on such things as the definition of a winery, including the 
ancillary activities that may be conducted thereon. 
The effect on 
the particular commissioners may not be the same as the effect on 
the general public in that property owners affected in a similar 
manner constitute a very limited number of people, all of whom 
must come before the Commission for approval of their projects. 
Very truly yours, 
'B~ 
County Counsel

Gentlemen: 
This office requests an opinion pursuant to Government Code 
Section 83114(b) as to the duty of certain Napa County Planning 
Commissioners to refrain from voting under the following 
conditions. 
At a regular meeting on October 7, 1987, the subject commissioners 
consented to this office requesting this opinion. 
Napa County is famous throughout the world for its wine industry. 
The total population of Napa County is approximately 102,000. 
Napa County is small, with a total of 513,000 acres of land, most 
of which is mountainous. 
Most of the wine industry is found in a 
narrow corridor along Hwy. 29 and the Los Carneros area in the 
southwestern part of the County_ 
This area encompasses only 11% 
of the County. 
Within this smaller area, there are now 154 
operating wineries of all sizes with several more either under 
construction or requesting approval. 
The wine industry, along 
with the one million plus tourists that it brings to this area, 
constitutes a major economic resource for the County. 
Many 
wineries own their own vineyards in the valley, others buy grapes 
om local vineyards.

Mr. Nord is also a 33% interest owner of a winery and 
vineyard located within the unincorporated area of the 
County, but does not participate in management. 
2. 
Guy Kay -- Vice-President in charge of Production 
Operations of Berringer Winery. 
Berringer's corporate 
business name is Wine World, Incorporated. They produce 
various wines at their winery within the city limits of 
the City of St. Helena, thus not subject to County 
regulations, but they own vineyards within the 
unincorporated area of the County. 
Mr. Kay receives a 
salary and participates in a plan for extra 
compensation to executives. 
This extra compensation is 
not made conditioned upon any particular level of 
profits. 
3. 
Linda McGrew is not employed by a winery, but her 
husband, as an independent contractor, publishes the 
Domaine Chandon Quarterly for the Domaine Chandon 
Winery. 
The Quarterly only applies to Domaine Chandon. 
The matters concerning wineries that come before the commissioners 
are as follows: 
1. 
Use permits for the construction or expansion of 
wineries. 
The Planning Commission is the final 
authority in this matter absent an appeal to the Board 
of Supervisors. 
In very few cases are there appeals 
from winery approvals. 
2. 
The commissioners make the determination regarding the 
requirement of an Environmental Impact Report and 
impose operating conditions and regulate improvements 
on wineries. 
3. 
The definition of a winery is a specific matter soon to 
come before the Commission. 
The definition will 
address to what extent other activities such as sales 
of wine-related equipment, gifts, and souvenirs would 
be permitted, and whether entertainment and restaurants 
or other food sales will be permi ted as a part of a 
nery. These ancillary ac ivities are a source of 
substantial profits. 
The Board of Supe visors must 
approve any definition. 
i 
ha 
s 
ut 
ra 
s 
he is ue 
hat the 
small population of Napa Coun 
ry small 
, and the 
heavy c 
centrat on 
wineri s and win ry-related activiti s 


Fair Practices Commission 
October 9, 1987 
Page 3 
makes it probable that the financial interests of corporations of 
which the commissioners are officers could be affected by their 
votes on such things as the definition of a winery, including the 
ancillary activities that may be conducted thereon. 
The effect on 
the particular commissioners may not be the same as the effect on 
the general public in that property owners affected in a similar 
manner constitute a very limited number of people, all of whom 
must come before the Commission for approval of their projects. 
Very truly yours, 
County Counsel

D:9040 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
R. Clifford Lober 
County Counsel, Napa County 
1195 Third street, Room 301 
Napa, CA 
94559-3001 
Dear Mr. Lober: 
October 27, 1987 
Re: 
87-267 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on October 23, 1987 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Lilly Spitz, an attorney in the 
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact" you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours, 
Diane M. Griffiths

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •

sections.question:
Do the economic interests of Planning Commission Members 
Nord, Kay and McGrew in the wine industry require their 
disqualification from participation in the following planning 
commission decisions: 
(1) 
creating a legal definition of the term "winery"; and 
(2) 
Requiring use permits, environmental impact reports, 
or other regulation of operations or improvements of wineries? 
1, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California 
Administrative Code. 
~/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with 
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. 
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.) 
~0807 • (916) 322~5660 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988
sections.conclusion:
1. 
All three planning commissioners must disqualify 
themselves from decisions regarding the definition of 
"winery." 
Should definitional issues become more narrowly 
focused, allowing for a more individual analysis of the 
foreseeable effects on the planning commissioners' economic 
interests, it is possible that some participation may be 
allowed in the future. 
2. 
All three planning commissioners must disqualify 
themselves from any decisions regarding use permits, 
environmental impact reports, or regulation of operation or 
improvements for their employers, sources of income or business 
interests. 
Additionally, they must disqualify themselves from 
decisions involving a competing winery or vintner when the 
competitor is the subject of a decision which could foreseeably 
have a material financial effect on the individual 
commissioner's economic interests.
sections.facts:
Napa County has a population of approximately 102,000 
people, and a total land area of 513,000 acres of land, most of 
which is mountainous. 
Napa's wine industry is world renowned 
and is situated in a narrow corridor encompassing 11% of the 
county's land. 
There are 154 operating wineries in the 
county. 
The wine industry, along with the one-mil 1 ion-plus 
tourists that it brings to the area, constitutes a major 
economic resource for the county. 
Three of the five planning commission members have ties to 
the wine industry: 
will Nord -- Vice-president in charge of viticultural 
operations at Domaine Chandon, a major facility with a 
restaurant, gift shop and musical entertainment located within 
the unincorporated area of the county. 
Mr. Nord is also a 33% 
owner of a winery and vineyard located in the county. 
Guy Kay -- Vice-president in charge of production 
operations of Beringer Winery. 
Beringer owns vineyards within 
the unincorporated area of the county. 
Beringer produces its 
wines at its winery in the City of st. Helena, which is outside 
the jurisdiction of the county. 
Mr. Kay receives a salary and 
participates in a plan for extra compensation to executives 
which is not conditioned upon any particular level of profits. 
Linda McGrew -- Her husband is an independent contractor 
who is under contract to publish the Domaine Chandon Quarterly 
for the Domaine Chandon winery. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
The matters concerning wineries that come before the 
planning commission include: 
1. Use permits for the construction or expansion of 
wineries. 
The planning commission is the final authority, 
absent an appeal to the board of supervisors. 
2. Determinations regarding whether to require 
environmental impact reports. 
3. possible imposition of conditions for operation of and 
improvements to wineries. 
4. Establishing the legal definition of 
"winery." 
Historically, the Napa County zoning code has permitted the 
operation of a winery in either the agriculture-watershed (AW) 
zone or in the agricultural preserve (AP) zone. 
The current 
zoning ordinance has, for years, defined a winery as a facility 
for the processing and storage of grapes and appurtenant 
facilities and uses. 
Under this rather broad definition, wineries have 
flourished in the Napa Valley and have engaged in numerous 
"appurtenant uses," including conducting tours, operating 
restaurants and delicatessens, providing picnic supplies and 
facilities, and selling souvenirs and memorabilia, not to 
mention the sale of wines. 
Currently pending before the planning commission is a 
proposal for a revised definition of permitted winery uses 
within the AW and AP zones. 
This proposal includes a 
requirement that some or all of the winery-related uses 
described above would be permitted only upon obtaining a 
conditional use permit. 
Among the various possible versions of the use permit 
requirement are proposals to "grandfather" in those uses which 
are currently in effect at the various wineries. 
Thus, those 
uses would be permitted to continue without the necessity of 
applying for a use permit. 
However, any future new uses of 
this type would be subject to the use permit requirement. 
Another version would require wineries with all such uses, 
whether ongoing or not, to apply for a use permit. 
If denied, 
the current use would be required to stop. 
It is also possible 
that the current use would be permitted, but would be 
conditioned on other requirements such as additional parking. 
A third version would "grandfather in" the basic winery 
operations, but require use permits for any of the 
"appurtenant" uses. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
Lastly, wine grape growers have proposed that the 
definition for a winery operating in these two zones include a 
requirement that at least 75% of all grapes utilized at the 
winery be grown in Napa County. 
In addition, under this 
proposed definition, no bottled or bulk wines could be brought 
into the county for use by Napa County wineries. 
The growers 
learned of a similar provision in the Santa Barbara County 
zoning ordinance and seek its inclusion in the revised Napa 
County ordinance. 
Vintners, on the other hand, have expressed opposition to 
the inclusion of these restrictions on their operations. 
They 
often use out-of-county grapes or bulk wines in the production 
of their blended or non-premium wines. 
Because these wines 
constitute a major portion of the wines made by Napa County 
wineries, the total percentage of out-of-county grapes may 
easily exceed 25%. 
In addition, several of the wineries store 
the wines which they have produced in Napa County in storage 
facilities outside the county. 
This is done for economic 
reasons. 
The vintners oppose a restriction on bringing this 
wine back into the county at a later date.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, 
participating in, or using their official positions to 
influence a governmental decision in which they know or have 
reason to know they have a financial interest. 
An official has 
a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on the official or a member of the official's 
immediate family, or on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than gifts and 
other than loans by a commercial lending institution 
in the regular course of business on terms available 
to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more 
in value provided to, received by or promised to the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
section 87103(a)-(d). 
All the planning commissioners are public officials, as 
defined in section 82048. 
Each of the three in question has 
one or more economic interests which could be materially 
affected by a decision of the planning commission. 
Mr. Nord is a vice-president with Domaine Chandon; thus 
Domaine Chandon is a source of income of $250 or more and a 
business entity in which he holds a position of management. 
(Sections 82005 and 82030.) 
Additionally, his 33-percent 
interest in a winery and vineyard in the county are both an 
investment interest in a business entity and an interest in 
real property which we assume are worth at least $1,000 each. 
(Sections 82033 and 82034.) 
Mr. Kay is vice-president in charge of production 
operations for Beringer Winery; thus the winery is a source of 
income of $250 or more and a business entity in which he holds 
a position of management. 
(Sections 82005 and 82030.) 
Ms. McGrew's husband's contractual relationship with 
Domaine Chandon makes Domaine Chandon a source of income to 
her, based on her community property share of the gross income 
received or promised. 
(Section 82030(a).) 
If her husband's 
gross income from Domaine Chandon was $500 or more in the past 
year, Domaine Chandon is a source of income of $250 or more to 
Ms. McGrew. 
In light of these various financial relationships, each of 
these public officials would be required to disqualify himself 
or herself if a decision of the planning commission would have 
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his or 
her economic interests, and the effect is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. 
Question #1. 
Foreseeability 
We will examine first whether the planning commissioners 
may vote on the definition of "winery." 
As was noted 
previously, public officials may not participate in 
governmental decisions which could foreseeably have a material 
financial effect on their economic interests in a manner 
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
The 
effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
sUbstantial likelihood that it will occur. 
Certainty is not 
required; however, if the effect is a mere possibility, it is 
not reasonably foreseeable. 
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
198, copy enclosed.) 
Deciding on the legal definition of "winery", and including 
therein any necessary language regarding the issuance of use 
permits, is an issue that will have a direct impact on the 
financial future of the growers and wineries in the county. 
Each of the proposals submitted for consideration to the 
planning commission would have a foreseeable effect on the 
economic interests of the three commissioners. 
Material Financial Effect 
It is necessary next to determine if the decisions 
regarding the definition of winery will materially affect the 
planning commissioners' economic interests. 
The effect of a 
decision is material if it is "significant." 
(Regulation 
18702(a), copy enclosed.) 
Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) 
contains monetary guidelines for determining whether the effect 
of a decision will be material. 
The test of materiality varies 
with the financial size of the business entity in which the 
public official has an economic interest. 
For example, an 
effect of only $10,000 on the gross revenues or assets of a 
small business is material, while a $1 million effect on the 
gross revenues or assets of a Fortune 500 company is material. 
The businesses in which the planning commissioners have 
economic interests range from Mr. Nord's local winery and 
vineyard, to Domaine Chandon which is a multi-million dollar 
international corporation. 
While we are not in the position to determine the precise 
magnitude of the effect of these decisions, a significant 
effect is likely because the ramifications of the various 
options are so substantial. 
For example, the proposal that the 
definition for winery include a requirement that at least 75% 
of all grapes utilized by wineries located in the county be 
grown in Napa County would greatly benefit Beringer, whose 
grapes, grown in the county, would be more in demand. 
The 
value of Beringer's assets would increase under such a 
definition. 
At the same time, this recommended definition 
would have a negative impact on Domaine Chandon because the 
winery's current practice of utilizing grapes from other 
regions for wine production would be severely limited. 
However, how much this would specifically affect Domaine 
Chandon's assets or increase its expenses is a determination 
you will have to make. 
On the other hand, the proposed "grandfathering" provision 
for wineries with "appurtenant uses" would greatly benefit 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
Domaine Chandon because of the many attractions currently in 
operation at the winery. 
Those wineries with little or no 
extraneous activity would be at a distinct disadvantage due to 
the proposed use permit requirement, while Domaine Chandon's. 
assets would increase in value. 
In light of the significant effect of a new definition of 
winery, we must conclude that the decision regarding the 
definition of winery will have a material financial effect on 
the economic interests of the planning commissioners. 
However, as the discussion regarding the definition of 
winery becomes more focused, the effects of the provisions 
under consideration will be more easily identified. 
At that 
time, it would be advisable to re-evaluate the specific effect 
of the proposed legislation on the economic interests of the 
individual planning commissioners, and apply the appropriate 
guidelines of Regulation 18702.2. 
It is possible that the 
language in the definition under discussion at that time would 
not have a material effect on each of the economic interests of 
the planning commissioners. 
Public Generally Exception 
The final question to be answered in determining whether 
the planning commissioners face a conflict-of-interest 
situation is whether the decisions regarding the definition of 
winery will affect their financial interests in a manner that 
is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Regulation 18703 (copy enclosed) provides that a material 
financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's 
interests is distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally unless the decision will affect the official's 
economic interest in substantially the same manner as it will 
affect all members of the public or a significant segment of 
the public. 
The "public," for purposes of this exception, 
includes the residents and businesses located within the 
jurisdiction of the planning commission. 
(See In re Owen 
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copy enclosed.) 
Thus, for purposes of 
the planning commission, the public includes all residents and 
businesses in the county. 
The decisions regarding the definition of "winery" clearly 
do not have the same or similar effect on the wine industry as 
they do on the general public. 
However, because the wine 
industry is a major industry in Napa County, one might assume 
that it constitutes a "significant segment of the public". 
Regulation 18703 specifically defines when an industry is a 
significant segment of the public: 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
... Except as provided herein, an industry, trade or 
profession does not constitute a significant segment 
of the general public. 
(a) 
In the case of an elected state officer, an 
industry, trade or profession constitutes a 
significant segment of the public generally. 
(b) 
In the case of any other elected official, 
an industry, trade or profession of which that 
official is a member may constitute a significant 
segment of the public generally if that industry, 
trade or profession is a predominant industry, trade 
or profession in the official's jurisdiction or in the 
district represented by the official. 
(c) 
An industry, trade or profession constitutes 
a significant segment of the public if the statute, 
ordinance or other provision of law which creates or 
authorizes the creation of the official's agency or 
office contains a finding and declaration, including 
an express reference to section 87103 of the 
Government Code, to the following effect: 
The Legislature [or other authority] declares 
that the individual[s] appointed to the office 
of 
is [are] intended to represent 
and further the interest of the [specified 
industry, trade or profession], and that such 
representation and furtherance will ultimately 
serve the public interest. 
ACcordingly, the 
Legislature [or other authority] finds that for 
purposes of persons who hold such office the 
[specified industry, trade or profession] is 
tantamount to and constitutes the public 
generally within the meaning of Section 87103 of 
the Government Code. 
(d) 
In the absence of an express finding and 
declaration of the type described in sUbsection (c) of 
this section, such an industry, trade or profession 
constitutes a significant segment of the public 
generally only if such a finding and declaration is 
implicit, taking into account the language of the 
statute, ordinance or other provision of law creating 
or authorizing the creation of the agency, the nature 
and purposes of the program, any applicable 
legislative history, and any other relevant 
circumstance. 
As you can see, Regulation 18703(a) applies to only state 
elected officials, and sUbsection (b) applies only to other 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
elected officials. 
Since the planning commissioners serve in 
appointive positions, these provisions do not apply to them. 
Moreover, the "predominant industry" language in Regulation 
18703(b) is generally reserved for the "company town" 
situation. (See, Holmer Advice Letter, No. A-86-051, copy 
enclosed. ) 
Finally, the ordinance establishing the planning commission 
does not include specific language or implicit findings that it 
is in the public interest to appoint planning commissioners 
with expertise in wine-related fields. 
Planning commissions 
are not typically the kind of agency envisioned for the 
exceptions allowed in Regulations 18704(c) and (d). 
These 
provisions are intended to apply to boards and commissions 
created for the purpose of furthering the interests of a 
particular industry, trade or profession. 
(See Consumers Union 
of united States v. California Milk producers-Advisory Board 
(1978) 82 Cal. App. 3d 433.) 
Therefore, the "public 
generally" exception does not apply, and disqualification is 
required. 
Question #2. 
The second question involves the planning commissioners' 
participation in decisions regarding environmental impact 
reports, use permits or other issues specific to individual 
wineries, vineyards or businesses within the jurisdiction of 
the planning commission. 
Where the decision in question 
involves a business in which the public official has an 
economic interest, the public official's participation is 
addressed in Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed), which states 
in pertinent part: 
... a public official shall not make, participate in 
making, or use his or her official position to 
influence a government decision if: 
(1) 
Any person (including a business 
entity) which has been a source of income 
(including gifts) to the official of $250 or more 
in the preceding 12 months appears before the 
official in connection with the decision; 
(2) 
Any business entity in which the 
official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$1,000 or more, or in which the official is an 
officer, director, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management, appears before 
the official in connection with the decision; 
**** 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
(b) 
A person or business entity appears before 
an official in connection with a decision when that 
person or entity either personally or by an agent: 
(1) 
Initiates the proceeding in which the 
decision will be made by filing an application, 
claim, appeal or similar request; 
(2) 
Is a named party in the proceeding 
concerning the decision before the official or 
the body on which the official serves. 
Regulation 18702.1. 
Thus, as to the type of decisions by the planning 
commission which involves an action related to a specific 
winery, such as the granting or denying of a use permit or 
imposing operating conditions, all three of the planning 
commissioners would have to disqualify themselves where their 
employer, source of income or investment interest appears 
before them. 
Another potential for disqualification arises where a 
competing winery or vintner appears before the planning 
commission seeking a use permit or other specific action. 
Because the entities seeking action or named in a proceeding 
are not economically tied to the public official, the 
provisions of Regulation 18702.1 do not apply to this type of 
situation. 
In such circumstances, the planning commissioners must 
determine whether the decision regarding the competitor will 
have a foreseeable material financial effect on their 
individual economic interests. 
The guidelines provided in 
Regulation 18702.2 and the analysis discussed under Question 
#1, above, would have to be used to determine whether there is 
a conflict-of-interest situation. 
(See also, Kay Advice 
Letter, No. A-83-157, copy enclosed.) 
Legally Required Participation 
The planning commission is made up of five members. 
Because three of the five planning commissioners have 
disqualifying financial interests regarding the decision on 
defining winery, their disqualification from participation 
leaves you without a quorum. 
section 87101 and Regulation 
18701 (copy enclosed) permit a public official to participate 
in a decision although he or she has a financial interest, but 
only when the official's participation is legally required and 
there exists no alternative source for the decision. 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988  
In order to take action on the winery definition issue, 
three of the five planning commissioners must participate. 
Accordingly, participation by one of the three disqualified 
planning commissioners is legally required. 
(See In re Huds.on 
(1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13. 
See also Advice Letters to Hopkins 
A-82-088, Rainville A-85-024, and Levinger A-87-061, copies 
enclosed. ) 
It is important to emphasize that the rule of "legally 
required participation" under the Act is more strict than the 
common law "rule of necessity". 
As was noted in In re Hudson, 
supra: 
While the common law rule of necessity is analogous to 
the concept of "legally required participation" 
contained in the Political Reform Act, we believe that 
the purposes of the Act are best served by a rule 
which minimizes participation in government decisions 
by officials with a conflict of interest. 
In re Hudson, supra at p 17. 
In accordance with the requirements of the Act, you may 
select only the number of disqualified public officials legally 
necessary to take action. 
In this case, three of the five 
planning commissioners constitute a quorum, which is the legal 
requirement for decision-making by the planning commission. 
A 
method of random selection, such as drawing lots, is the 
preferred method for deciding which one of the three 
disqualified councilmembers may participate in these decisions. 
I trust this response provides sufficient guidance 
regarding the responsibilities of the planning commissioners 
under the Act. 
If, in the future, you have questions 
concerning the specific components of these important issues 
facing the planning commission, please feel free to contact me 
at (916) 322-5901. 
DMG:LS:plh 
Enclosures

QUESTION: Do the economic interests of Planning Commission Members 
Nord, Kay and McGrew in the wine industry require their 
disqualification from participation in the following planning 
commission decisions: 
(1) 
creating a legal definition of the term "winery"; and 
(2) 
Requiring use permits, environmental impact reports, 
or other regulation of operations or improvements of wineries? 
1, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California 
Administrative Code. 
~/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with 
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. 
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.) 
~0807 • (916) 322~5660 


R. Clifford Lober 
January 6, 1988

CONCLUSION: 1. 
All three planning commissioners must disqualify 
themselves from decisions regarding the definition of 
"winery." 
Should definitional issues become more narrowly 
focused, allowing for a more individual analysis of the 
foreseeable effects on the planning commissioners' economic 
interests, it is possible that some participation may be 
allowed in the future. 
2. 
All three planning commissioners must disqualify 
themselves from any decisions regarding use permits, 
environmental impact reports, or regulation of operation or 
improvements for their employers, sources of income or business 
interests. 
Additionally, they must disqualify themselves from 
decisions involving a competing winery or vintner when the 
competitor is the subject of a decision which could foreseeably 
have a material financial effect on the individual 
commissioner's economic interests.

Clifford Lober County Counsel Napa County 1195 Third st., Room 301 Napa, CA 94559-3001 Dear Mr. Lober: January 6, 1988 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance; Our File No. 1-87-267 You have requested advice concerning the responsibilities of Napa County Planning Commission Members will Nord, Guy Kay and Linda McGrew under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ Because your request is more of a general inquiry than a request for advice as to a specific action pending before the planning commission, we treat your request as one for informal assistance.~/ QUESTIONS Do the economic interests of Planning Commission Members Nord, Kay and McGrew in the wine industry require their disqualification from participation in the following planning commission decisions: (1) creating a legal definition of the term "winery"; and (2) Requiring use permits, environmental impact reports, or other regulation of operations or improvements of wineries? 1/ Government Code sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Administrative Code section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Administrative Code. ~/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3), copy enclosed.) 428 J Street, Suite 800 .. P.O. Box 807 ~ Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916) 322~5660 R. Clifford Lober January 6, 1988 Page 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. All three planning commissioners must disqualify themselves from decisions regarding the definition of "winery." Should definitional issues become more narrowly focused, allowing for a more individual analysis of the foreseeable effects on the planning commissioners' economic interests, it is possible that some participation may be allowed in the future. 2. All three planning commissioners must disqualify themselves from any decisions regarding use permits, environmental impact reports, or regulation of operation or improvements for their employers, sources of income or business interests. Additionally, they must disqualify themselves from decisions involving a competing winery or vintner when the competitor is the subject of a decision which could foreseeably have a material financial effect on the individual commissioner's economic interests. FACTS Napa County has a population of approximately 102,000 people, and a total land area of 513,000 acres of land, most of which is mountainous. Napa's wine industry is world renowned and is situated in a narrow corridor encompassing 11% of the county's land. There are 154 operating wineries in the county. The wine industry, along with the one-mil 1 ion-plus tourists that it brings to the area, constitutes a major economic resource for the county. Three of the five planning commission members have ties to the wine industry: will Nord -- Vice-president in charge of viticultural operations at Domaine Chandon, a major facility with a restaurant, gift shop and musical entertainment located within the unincorporated area of the county. Mr. Nord is also a 33% owner of a winery and vineyard located in
